label: Vision Transformer
definition: An architecture that applies the transformer directly to images by cutting each image into a grid of patches, embedding the patches as tokens, and processing them with self-attention, dispensing with convolution.

body.text: A vision transformer, abbreviated ViT, is the application of the [transformer](transformer) architecture, originally built for language, to images. Its central idea is disarmingly simple: chop the image into a grid of fixed-size patches, typically sixteen by sixteen pixels, flatten and linearly project each patch into a vector, and treat the resulting sequence of patch vectors exactly as a transformer treats a sequence of word tokens. The model then processes this sequence with stacked [self-attention](self-attention) layers and reads out a prediction. The 2020 paper that introduced it carried the fitting title "An Image Is Worth 16x16 Words".

The vision transformer matters because it broke the long assumption that vision required convolution. For nearly a decade the [convolutional neural network](convolutional-neural-network) was the only competitive architecture for images, its built-in priors of locality and translation equivariance seemingly essential. ViT showed that, given enough data, a near-generic transformer with almost none of those priors could match or beat convolutional models on [image classification](image-classification). That result unified vision and language under one architecture, letting both fields share innovations, training recipes, and scaling insight, and it is what made cross-modal models like [clip](clip) and modern multimodal large language models natural to build.

Mechanically, the patch step is a form of [image tokenization](image-tokenization): it turns a continuous image into a discrete sequence of tokens that attention can consume. Because attention is permutation-invariant and has no inherent sense of where a patch sat in the image, a learned [positional encoding](positional-encoding) is added to each patch embedding to restore spatial location. A special learnable classification token is often prepended to the sequence, and its output state, having attended to every patch, serves as the image-level summary fed to the classifier. Every patch can attend to every other patch from the very first layer, giving ViT a global receptive field immediately, in contrast to a convolutional network that builds up to global context only through depth.

The trade-off against the convolutional network is instructive. A convolutional network bakes in helpful assumptions, so it learns efficiently from modest data; ViT bakes in fewer, so on small datasets it tends to overfit and underperform, but on very large datasets it keeps improving past where convolutional models plateau, because it is free to learn whatever spatial relationships the data actually contain. Hybrid designs and strong [data augmentation](data-augmentation) narrow this gap, and the resulting representations transfer broadly across detection, segmentation, and retrieval.
